content_html:
Transactions & ACID
Data Warehousing & ETL
INF10025  Data Management and Analytics  Lecture 11 – Part 1

Database Transactions
A database transaction is an important concept of any RDBMS
We are not going to cover this topic in much depth.
However it is important to know what a database transaction is.
Database transactions are covered in much more detail in the unit named Database Systems.

A simple Banking system with customers
Imagine we have a small Banking Database
It has a customer table
CUSTOMER (Cust Id, Name, Balance)
As a customer deposits or withdraws money, the balance is updated.
E.g. Balance is updated when $50 is deposited.
Problem. We are not storing a history of deposits or withdrawals.E.g. We cannot list the most recent 3 deposits by this customer.
CustId
Name

A simple Banking system with customers
The customer & bank would both like to keep a history of all banking events.
Deposits
Withdrawals
Interest payments (deposits)   …
The situation is improved if bank uses another table named BankEvent
BankEvent (Eventid, EventDateTime, Custid, Event, Amount)
This example shows the event when $50 is deposited by customer 101

Each time a banking event occurs, these tables must be updated
The BankEvent table
The Customer table

Database – must be consistent
Obviously, whenever a banking event occurs, two tables must be updated
Customer table
BankEvent table
To do this, we must execute exactly two sequential SQL commands.
An Insert statement followed by
An Update statement
Both statements must be executed.
This takes the database from one consistent state to another
The sum total of all Bank Events is equal to the Customer Balance
DBMSs have a way of ensuring that all tables are updated appropriately. This is done via a database transaction.

Transactions
A databases transaction is typically made up of multiple SQL statements.
A transaction is viewed as a "logical unit of work"
E.g. Multiple rows in multiple tables may be updated
A successful transaction guarantees that
ALL of the operations of that transaction are completed
This data is committed
All changes made by the transaction are permanent
An unsuccessful transaction guarantees that
None of the operations of that transaction are completed
This data is rolled back (not committed)
All changes made by the transaction are cancelled
The DBMS ensures that every transaction is either Committed or Rolled-back

Transactions
The banking database uses a transaction whenever a bank event occurs
This ensures that the tables Customer and BankEvent are:
Both updated or
Neither updated.
This is useful when
A system error occurs such as power failure happens
A business rule is broken (a customer attempts to withdraw more money than the current customers balance).

Transactions that fail to complete
There is always danger when executing multiple SQL statements
The 1st statement is executed but the 2nd statement is not executed.
INSERT INTO BankEvent (Eventid, EventDateTime, Custid, Event, Amount)
VALUES (7002, 1 June 9am, 101, Withdraw, 75);
     power failure 
UPDATE   Customer SET          Balance = Balance - 75 WHERE    CustId = 101;
The DBMSs detects that the transaction was not completed.
The DBMS automatically performs a roll-back of the transaction.
It is as though none of the inserts or updates took place
Not executed

Transactions that break business rules
The bank has a check constraint on the customer table
It checks that a customer balance cannot be less than zero.
If an update statement causes the balance to be negative, then an exception / error is raised. An auto rollback occurs.
Customer 101 has balance of $20.   He tries to withdraw $50.
START TRANSACTION
INSERT INTO BankEvent (Eventid, EventDateTime, Custid, Event, Amount)
VALUES (7061, 4 June 2pm, 101, Withdraw, 50);
UPDATE CUSTOMER SET BALANCE = BALANCE – 50;   *
END TRANSACTION
* A Check Constraint exception error is raised by the DBMS       The Transaction is automatically rolled-back
Note: The above code is not complete nor fully SQL compliant

Transaction
When a customers performs a Deposit or a Withdrawal then
Both tables must be updated    OR
Neither table is updated
We must never update only one of the tables.

ACID Properties: Atomicity, Consistency
All database transactions must adhere to the ACID properties
Atomicity
Consistency
Isolation
Durability

ACID Properties: Atomicity, Consistency
All database transactions must adhere to the ACID properties
Atomicity
A transaction is indivisible
The transaction is performed in its entirety or not at all.
All statements are performed successfully  OR
None of the statements are performed
Implemented by COMMIT and ROLLBACK
Consistency
A transaction transforms the database from one consistent state to another consistent state.
Done by executing all statements (or none) of a transaction.
No such thing as 'half a transaction'

ACID Properties: Isolation, Durability
All transactions must adhere to the ACID properties
Isolation (discussed in INF20010 Database Systems )
Transactions are isolated from one another.
A transaction that works correctly in isolation (by itself)is guaranteed to work correctly when run multiple times at the same time or with other transactions
Implemented by locking and/or multiversioning
Durability (discussed in INF20010)
Ensures the results of a committed transaction can never be  lost
Even in the event of hardware, memory, system failure
Implemented by transaction logging and recovery

Consistency Summary
When a transaction is
Entirely Completed    OR
Entirely reversed,
it means we achieve a consistent database state
Transactions take a database from one consistent database state to another consistent database state
A consistent database state is a fundamental principle that all databases follow
Failure to do this
Is a serious problem
Must be avoided through good development techniques
May be illegal

Transaction: Summary
In summary:
A database transaction is a sequence of database operations that together achieve a single task.
A transaction transforms the database from one consistent state to another.
A transaction must be executed in its entirety or not at all.
If an a transaction cannot be completed successfully, then the transaction must be rolled back
